texts.full_text: MLCH simply means "sacrifice by fire". Most ancient peoples, such as the Iranians, made fire sacrifices to their gods. Lucia Chiavola-Birnbaum writes in Dark Mother that African- Phoenician Carthage has been seen as "degenerate" not "vigorous" by European scholars because they lived at peace with the Berbers and did not war against and enslave peoples. Their abilities and skills have also been belittled. In fact, the Phoenicians-Canaanites invented the alphabet, and were great architects, shipbuilders and skilled artisans. Tanit's Mysteries [Monica Sjöö, 2002] They were also great seafarers who founded towns and cities and brought cultural skills to many lands. They were furniture-makers and made delicate ivory inlays and carved great ivory screens. They made translucent glass and beautiful and purple textiles and much else. But they did not build palaces and temples nor did they establish kingship and hierarchies, and they remained faithful to the Goddess and their matrifocal past. Therefore they stand condemned! We know very little about Tanit's mystery religion and its rituals and rites. The Romans burnt Carthage to the ground, with its libraries and sacred places, and massacred the entire population at the end of the Punic wars in 146 BCE. There was however one place in Carthegenian Spain that escaped the general destruction and plunder by the Romans, and that was Ibiza of the Balearic islands, which is where I will turn next on my search for Tanit. Part 2 - Tanit on Ibiza will be in the next issue of GA! Home New book! See the description from FME help: To compare two files with FME and find differences between them, especially when dealing with CSV files containing similar feature types but different field names, you can utilize the Change Detector transformer in FME. This tool allows you to identify variations in attribute values or even geometry between the two datasets. Here's a step-by-step guide on how to use it: 1. Open FME Desktop: Launch your FME workspace where you'll build your comparison workflow. 2. Add Readers: Add two reader transformers to your workspace, one for each CSV file you want to compare. Ensure they are properly configured to read the files correctly. 3. Change Detector: - Add a Change Detector transformer after both readers. - Connect the output features from both reader transformers to the input features of the Change Detector. - In the settings for the Change Detector, you have options to compare attribute values and/or geometry. - To compare only the attribute values (fields with different names), ensure that "Compare Attributes Only" is checked. Specifically, those living alone were more likely to have DRP related to the category 'Taking too little' (adjusted OR = 2.32 [1.28-4.20], p = 0.006) and which involved the use of HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors (adjusted OR = 2.78 [1.16-6.69], p = 0.022). texts.full_text: r higher ensures adequate hepatic function and minimizes the risk of small-for-size syndrome, which can lead to inadequate blood flow through the transplanted organ. In cases where this critical threshold is not met due to anatomical constraints during surgery—such as when a left lobe graft from an adult donor (typically 30-45% of total liver volume) does not provide sufficient hepatic mass—the recipient may develop complications related to small-for-size syndrome. These can include hyperbilirubinemia, coagulopathy, and ascites. To manage the hemodynamic consequences associated with a smaller graft size in left lobe living donor transplants (LDLT), surgeons sometimes create intraoperative portosystemic shunts like the mesocaval or hemiportocaval types. These serve to reduce portal pressure by diverting some of the blood flow from the high-pressure hepatic artery and into a lower resistance venous system, thus alleviating congestion in the graft. However, these temporary measures can lead to complications if left unaddressed postoperatively once adequate regeneration has occurred or when they become unnecessary. One such complication is persistent shunt patency leading to portal hypotension which may compromise long-term liver function and cause other systemic issues like encephalopathy due to excessive diversion of blood flow. In this context, endovascular closure techniques have emerged as a minimally invasive solution for managing these complications without the need for reoperation. Utilizing devices such as coils or occlusion balloons delivered through percutaneous access under fluoroscopic guidance allows precise and controlled shunt obliteration while monitoring hemodynamic stability in real-time. The decision to proceed with endovascular closure is typically made based on clinical assessment, imaging studies (such as Doppler ultrasound), liver function tests results post-transplantation. The procedure aims at optimizing portal venous perfusion without compromising systemic circulation or causing undue stress on the regenerating graft tissue. texts.full_text: of particular individuals or groups for risk, danger of deterioration in conditions or poor outcomes or achievements. Several factors may be seen as causing or influencing vulnerability, for instance: physical (e.g. sickness, disability), emotional/psychological (e.g. mental illness, immaturity, dependence), material (e.g. poverty, homelessness, health care, education), and social (lack of support by family or peer group, absence of guidance in difficult situations, and immediate risks from the environment). As such, vulnerability may be approached from different viewpoints, for instance individual conditions and behaviours, structural and systemic material conditions or also social relations and insecurity, and thus needs to be seen as a multidimensional concept and in relational terms. Every person in whatever context may feel vulnerable at some point in life, however, some individuals and groups are more vulnerable than others. The United Nations distinguish three different groups of people that are vulnerable, to what and forwhat reasons: 1. Vulnerable due to their personal situation: This includes individuals with physical or mental disabilities, chronic illnesses, or those in advanced age. Their vulnerability might stem from reduced mobility, dependence on others for care, or limited access to resources and services. 2. Vulnerable due to economic and social conditions: Poverty, lack of education, unemployment, and social exclusion can significantly increase an individual's vulnerability. These conditions often limit access to basic needs like food, shelter, healthcare, and education, making individuals more susceptible to exploitation, violence, and poor health outcomes. 3. Vulnerable due to environmental factors and external shocks: Natural disasters, conflict, political instability, and climate change can render populations vulnerable. These events can disrupt daily life, displace communities, and limit access to essential services, exacerbating existing vulnerabilities and creating new ones. Understanding these dimensions of vulnerability is crucial for developing effective policies and interventions that address the root causes and mitigate the impacts

texts.full_text: and not that uncommon. In this study, researchers studied the sex lives of 170 heterosexual college students. To be eligible for the study, all participants had to be sexually active and currently single, but have experienced a breakup at some point during the last year. The sample predominately consisted of White females who were in their freshman year. All participants completed a weekly diary for three months in which they were regularly asked how distressed they felt about their most recent breakup, as well as whether they had sex with anyone and the reason(s) they had sex. Participants had several reasons they could choose from, including rebound reasons (“to help you ‘get over’ your ex-partner and the breakup”) and revenge reasons (“to ‘get back at’ your ex-partner”). Most participants (66.5%) reported having sex at least once over the course of the three-month study. Of those sexual encounters, 20% involved sex with the ex, 54% involved sex with a former partner (who was not the most recent ex), and 26% involved sex with a new partner or a With regard to the reasons people reported having sex, about one-quarter of participants said they did so for revenge reasons and about one-third for rebound reasons. Those who had been “dumped” were the most likely to report these sexual motives--those who initiated the breakup themselves were less inclined to have sex for these reasons. Perhaps not surprisingly, the tendency to report havinG sex for revenge or rebound reasons declined over time, which coincided with a decrease in feelings of distress about the breakup. This suggests that sex is usually a relatively coping response. These results are limited from the standpoint that they primarily speak to the experiences of young heterosexual women who have just gone off to college after breaking up with their high school boyfriends. texts.full_text: n the river. For awhile it was used as a snowmobile crossing but there are signs posted now that the bridge has been deemed unstable and it's not even wise to walk across it. I didn't and maintained a healthy distance while taking pictures of what was obviously a worn deck. All things considered, i would have to say that the Randall Bridge aka Burrington Bridge was my favorite of those in Lyndon in spite of its beat and weathered condition. Or maybe it was because of its beat and weathered condition! Finally leaving Lyndon behind I drove east on Route 2 towards New Hampshire in search of the Mount Orne Bridge which spans the Connecticut River between Lunenberg, Vermont and Lancaster, New Hampshire. The Mount Orne bridge was built in 1911 but was not the first bridge to span the river at this point. The first bridge was built in either the 1860's or 1870's and was operated as a toll bridge until it was destroyed by a log jam in 1908. For the next two years a ferry service operated between Lunenberg and Lancaster until the Mount Orne bridge was completed. The bridge uses William Howe's wood and steel design and consists of two spans with a total length of 266 feet. The bridge, like the Cornish-Windsor Bridge that I wrote about earlier, is technically a New Hampshire covered bridge as the state line is at the western shore of the river and it only "touches" Vermont but I wasn't going to squabble as I really liked the weathered look of it. texts.full_text: y. Ninety-four subcutaneous arterio-venous fistulas were created for haemodialysis in 83 patients. Seventy-one patients eventually received well-functioning fistulas. The most common complication was thrombosis at the suture line. Thus, 8 primary fistulas clotted within 24 hours of surgery and 5 clotted later. Eleven patients were reoperated with a Eleven patients were reoperated with a variety of corrective measures, including end-to-side AV anastomosis revision, removal of nonpatent segments, and excision of fibrotic tissue at the suture line. Five of these reoperations resulted in functional fistulas, while six cases required further interventions such as polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) graft placement or central venous stent placement due to persistent outflow tract obstruction or central venous stenosis. Of the 71 successful fistulas, 64 matured within 4–8 weeks, whereas seven required balloon angioplasty to resolve stenotic lesions. The study highlights that early thrombosis was associated with inadequate proximal outflow vein diameter (<5 mm), hypotensive episodes postoperatively, and technical errors i

texts.full_text: inactivation kinetics. The aimThe aim of this study is to develop and validate a one-step global parameter estimation method for the thermal inactivation kinetics of Bacillus sporothermodurans spores. The method is designed to accurately and efficiently estimate the thermal inactivation parameters of this spore-forming bacterium under a variety of thermal conditions, including both static and dynamic thermal processes. The parameters of interest, including the thermal death time (D-value), the z-value (the temperature increase required to reduce the thermal death time by a factor of 10), and the shoulder and tail parameters, will be estimated to provide a comprehensive understanding of the thermal inactivation behavior of the spores. The study will also evaluate the robustness and reliability of the one-step global parameter estimation method, and the results will be compared to those obtained from traditional, step-by-step parameter estimation methods to highlight the method's advantages in terms of accuracy and efficiency. The findings will be crucial for the food industry to design and

PER CURIAM:* Appellant Luis Angel Maldonado challenges his sentence for being present in the United States illegally after having been deported following conviction for a felony, in violation of 8 U.S.C. § 1326(a) and (b). First he asserts that the district court erred by finding that his prior Texas convictions for burglary of a habitation were crimes of violence under U.S.S.G. § 2L1.2(b)(1)(A)(ii). * Pursuant to 5TH CIR. R. 47.5, the court has determined that this opinion should not be published and is not precedent except under the limited circumstances set forth in 5TH CIR. R. 47.5.4. The district court did not err. See United States v. Garcia-Mendez, 420 F.3d 454 (5th Cir. 2005), cert. denied, 126 S. Ct. 1398 (2006). Maldonado’s argument that we failed to apply the categorical analysis of Taylor v. United States, 495 U.S. 575 (1990), in Garcia-Mendez is essentially an argument that Garcia-Mendez was incorrectly decided, and it is unavailing. See Burge v. Parish of St. Tammany, 187 F.3d 452, 466 (5th Cir. 1999). Similarly, Maldonado asserts that the district court plainly erred by assessing criminal history points pursuant to § 4A1.1(f) for two prior burglary of a habitation convictions, based on a finding that they were crimes of violence under § 4B1.2(a). Burglary of a habitation in Texas is the equivalent of the enumerated offense of “burglary of a dwelling” under § 4B1.2(a)(2), and therefore it is a crime of violence under that section. See United States v. Hornsby, 88 F.3d 336, 339 (5th Cir. 1996). Maldonado’s assertion that Hornsby does not control because we did not apply Taylor is not persuasive. See Burge, 187 F.3d at 466. texts.full_text: five alternative places of MAP precipitation application for leather tanning wastewater, the following experimental setup was employed: 1. Instead of plain settling: In this method, after pretreatment steps like coagulation and flocculation, instead of allowing the solid particles to settle naturally (plain settling), magnesium ammonium phosphate is added. 2. After plain settling: Here, the wastewater undergoes natural settlement first before MAP precipitation occurs for further removal of suspended solids and dissolved pollutants such as ammonia nitrogen from leather tanning effluents. 3. After polyelectrolyte added plain settling: In this scenario, a flocculating agent (polyelectrolytes) is introduced into the wastewater to enhance particle aggregation before allowing it settle naturally for initial solid removal followed by MAP precipitation treatment. 4. Within chemical precipitation: This involves integrating magnesium ammonium phosphate within conventional coagulation and flocculation steps where metal salts are added to destabilize suspended particles, promoting their agglomeration which is then further treated with the addition of MAP precipitant for additional nitrogen removal from wastewater effluents. 5. After biological treatment: In this approach, biodegradation processes (such as activated sludge or biofiltration) precede chemical precipitation to reduce organic content and subsequently ammonium-loaded water passes through magnesium ammonium phosphate process for final nutrient recovery before discharge into the environment. Each of these experimental conditions was subjected under standardized procedures. The results were then compared in terms of nitrogen removal efficiency, MAP yield, sludge volume index (SVI), pH level adjustments required and overall cost implications to determine which application method offers superior ammonium reduction while maintaining economic feasibility for industrial-scale wastewater treatment. Overall findings from this study would provide insights into the optimal placement of magnesium ammonium phosphate precipitation in leath

We could tell FERC -- but then FERC wouldn;t do anything anyway -----Original Message----- From: CRCommunications [mailto:CRCommunications@caiso.com] Sent: Thursday, August 30, 2001 3:19 PM To: ISO Market Participants; SC Settlements Contacts Subject: CAISO Notice - June Preliminary Market Invoice Cash Distribution Deferral ISO Market Participants SC Settlement Contacts As you receive this message. The CCGT plants have been generating a lot of energy for the last few days. The NOC has received 6 different volumes at hourly resolutions, and all these values are greater than 5 MW every day from August to September during summer times. These outputs come under Plant Volume Adjustment which is an adjustment mechanism in-place that provides market-relevant congestion-free exchange points of bundled power for interested parties. texts.full_text: it's best to avoid chewing gum and eating hard, sticky food that might cause it to come loose. A Word About Tongue Thrusting Like thumb sucking, tongue thrusting is a normal behavioral pattern in young children. It's actually part of the natural infantile swallowing pattern, which will normally change on its own — by the age of six, in most children. If the pattern doesn't change, however, it can lead to problems similar to those caused by thumb sucking: namely, problems with tooth alignment and skeletal development. Fortunately, this problem can be successfully treated with a habit appliance that's very similar to a fixed palatal crib. Traditional approaches often require computation of intractable normalizing constants, which becomes computationally prohibitive on large lattices. Our proposed method circumvents this issue through strategic decomposition and conditional independence exploitation. The technique recursively divides a lattice into manageable sublattice segments while preserving essential dependencies via local interactions \cite{Decomposition}. This hierarchical approach allows for efficient computation of marginal probabilities without evaluating the global normalizing constant, achieving linear complexity $O(N)$ with respect to pixel count N. Empirical evaluations on Potts models show significant improvements over traditional methods that rely solely upon exact likelihood calculations across expansive lattice structures. Through this innovative methodological advancement in MRF estimation techniques targeting large-scale lattices \cite{pottsmodels}, we contribute a computationally efficient and scalable solution capable of handling the inherent complexities associated with spatially correlated data on extensive scales.
